sentences.text: It was giving out orders, everybody, every Jew from old to young, must enter the ghetto.
If they don't enter the ghetto by August 15, everybody is going to be shot.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] microphones, loudspeakers, and a lot of paper was glued down all over on the telephone poles and houses.
It was all printed, you know, forms, you know.
And every house glued on.
Every person's door.
And all the time constantly on the radios and they drove around with trucks, and big, you know, loudspeakers in every street.
All Jews must-- must come by the 15, and report themselves, and enter the ghetto.
And it was a lot of shootings, a lot of people were shot right away in different streets.
And right away the fear became big, and there was no way out.
You couldn't hide yourself, and you couldn't stay in your house.
If you stayed in your house, a gentile neighbor would come in, right away and say, why don't you go to ghetto.
I'm calling the Germans, Lithuanians.
The Lithuanians, if one wanted to hide himself in Kovno, on a side street, not to go in the ghetto, a Lithuanian neighbor would say right away, are you still here?
It's already the 14th.
Tomorrow you've got to go.
Because he was waiting already the Jew to leave all the furniture.
Not everybody could take everything with them.
There was no transportation to move, or to take things.
So you took your valuables, clothes, soft things.
But furniture, you hardly could drag it.
Jewish people didn't have, you know, trucks or things like that.
You had to hire, or you had to hire people with horses.
So everybody left their homes, and they all entered, by the 15th, the ghetto.
We had notification, almost the Germans came in on the 25th.
I think it took, like, 3, 4 weeks.
It was like, free, you know.
And then they started building the fences already.
They had the plan made, where the ghetto would be.
And they started grabbing Jews and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they start digging the holes where the poles are going to be.
My mother didn't want to prepare.
She didn't want to believe that we've got to leave our house that she worked so hard.
And there were poor people killed that built our house.
That she's going to leave the house.
She even start to cry, let them kill me.
I'm not going to go.
And we begged her, and we kissed her.
And the last ones to enter the ghetto there was, I think, our family.
Because our house, the real house where we lived, was not far from the ghetto, where we were told that we are going to live.

Because when I was in the ghetto, I could look through the fence, and see my house through the fence, and see gentile people already living in my house, a family, and the children were playing croquet that my father used to turn for the toy stores before the war.
And he used to sell the croquets.
So when we left the ghetto, we didn't take the croquets with us.
We knew we are not going to play croquet.
We are not going to sell croquets.
So we left a big stack of croquets-- croquet.
And when I used to look out for my house where I lived in the ghetto, and looked through the wires, while the guards were watching us, you know.
I didn't escape.
He knew I was not going escape, but I was just looking through the fence, I saw the gentile children playing croquet.
And I really cried.
How old were you?
I really cried.
I was 17 years old.
I cried with bitter tears.
And I said to my brother, I didn't want to tell my mother.
Abraham, look.
Look through the fence, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chasing the balls, knocking with the hammer the croquets, the children.
That's right.
That was the situation.
We took only soft pillows, blankets, clothes, candle holders, candlesticks, the silver ones, religious things, and whatever food we had.
That's all we took.
We had like potatoes, green peas, a little food, what we had, whatever the household had.
And we didn't have too much.
Because when we went into the ghetto, we were already 2-3, June, July, we were already rationed, and we couldn't get no food.
So we had very little food, only from some gentile people which we traded.
My mother said, Maishke, go give that farmer my gold earrings, and bring me a bag of green peas.
And my mother said, green peas, we don't have beef.
My mother was-- as I told you-- as I told you before, my mother was like a doctor with all her remedies.
She says, green peas is almost the same thing like you eat beef.
So I don't want no fancy things for my earrings, like a chicken or a piece of other food.
But potatoes and green peas.
She says, if you can get, get me as much as you can.
So we used to trade, and some of the farmers, you know, before we went into the ghetto, was very anxious to get Jewish gold rings, silver.
And the farmers from far away, from miles and miles away, from little towns, from little villages.
And you know, the rumors that the Jews are buying bread and green peas and food, and look what I just got.
I got earrings.
I got rings.
I got silver.
I got this.
So a lot of farmers start to sneak a little, before the ghetto.
But then the ghetto was closed, you couldn't bring anything.
Because the-- because the guards would shoot.
Yes.
She brought in, the jewelry, what she had.
And you know, she had materials, textile, you know.
Because my father used to work for textile factories.
So sometimes they used to give him a bonus.
And my mother bought, you know, she liked to sew a lot.
She used to make her own clothes for us.
She was a very good of sewing up things, you know.
She was a very talented woman, my mother.
So she had all the time materials, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] textile materials.
And this, when the war broke out, we took call the textile materials into the ghetto.
And that helped a lot in the ghetto, when later when I'm going to tell you.
When we used to take-- because they used to take us out-- out of the ghetto, with guards, to work on the airport, when the Germans were building.
So quietly we used to trade with gentile people, give them a piece of material for a suit, and we used to get a bag 15 or 20 pounds or 10 kilos of potatoes.
You said, in the house where you were told you were going to live--
Yes.
That was already a Jewish Committee, already was-- already established.
Before the ghetto was closed in, it was organized already Jewish community, which were educated people, like Dr.
Elkes, very prominent people from the Zionist organizations, a lot of doctors, like big Zionist Rabinowitz, from the iron wholesale stores.
He was a very prominent man in the ghetto, Rabinowitz.
And he knew my father very well.
And we were saved a lot of times by that Mr.
Rabinowitz.
Because my father used to do for them, certain turnings.
So actually in the ghetto, and it was a lot of selections to be taken out from Lithuania to Estonia, to other places.
And we had to make the lists.
The Jewish Committee had to deliver to the Germans, 1,000 Jews, 500 Jews.
And the committee didn't have no choice.
So they made lists with names, and they knew already in each house the names of the families.
It was already organized in the committee.
Exactly.
It's in German.
It's called a Judenrat.
On this committee was Dr.
Elkes, [NON-ENGLISH], [?
Brick, ?] Mr.
Brick, ?] Rabinowitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [?
Gershowitz, ?] Dr.
Greenberg, ?] Dr.
Berman, ?] and-- Dr.
Rabinowitz-- Dr.
Kaplan, ?] a woman doctor, Mrs.
Kaplan, ?] and a lot more people.
They were on the committee.
And they were the leaders.
But they had an order, from the Gestapo, to the leaders, so many people, nothing they could do.

And truthfully, the committee, the Judenrat like you say, for a long time didn't know 100% if Jews were taken out from the ghetto, that they were killed, till [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] on the-- one of the major selections, when they took out the 1,800 people.
And the clothes from these people there were trucks, driven by [NON-ENGLISH] guards.
When the people were shot, their clothes were taken in from the fortress, then the Jewish Committee knew that anytime they take out people, they are going to be killed.
But a lot of times they took out people not to be killed.
They took people in different sections, in different parts of Lithuania, for slave labor.
Like they took people to Kadan.
They took people to other little villages and states in Lithuania to do labor work.
But when they took out these people, it was a different atmosphere, then when they took out people to kill.
When they took people to kill, the SS, the way they acted was in a different manner.
A lot of them were drunk.
A lot of them knew that these 1,500 people are going to be dead in five or six hours, like cattle in a slaughter house.
So they treated these people, you know, with the rifle butts over the heads, over the shoulders.
March fast!
[SPEAKING GERMAN] It was a different type of treatment then people go to-- when they took people out for labor.
Because they figured, no use crippling the people when they take them to labor.
They're not going to be able to work.
But when the commanders, the Gestapo came, and they knew already that these type people are going to-- these people are going to be killed, these people were treated like cattle to a slaughterhouse.
So the committee, the Jewish Committee knew most of the time already.
But they didn't have no choice.
Dr.
Elkes couldn't say, kill me.
I'm not going to give out no more Jews.
So they would find another leader, a Jewish man who wants to save his life.
It couldn't help.
It's nothing against the Jewish Committee.
Nobody of the Jews have any anger against the Jewish Committee, especially in Lithuania, because they couldn't help themselves.
It was almost like a lotto.
They took these, these, and then-- and I'm sure they wiped their tears from their-- from their faces.
Because they had to make a list to give out 800 people, or 2,000 people, or 500 people.
Because it was not plain people who did this type of work-- this type committee work.
It was all distinguished, educated, good-hearted people.
I'm not talking about the Jewish police in the ghetto.
They were already a different type of people, you know, some of them were real-- I mean poor elements.
That, after the ghetto was established, they needed to know somebody Jewish people, you know, to keep control.
One Jew shouldn't rob from the other Jew.
So-- and the Jewish police had to do work for the committee.
If the Germans wanted that and that street Jews to come to that place, to go to work to the airfield, the Jewish police used to knock in the doors.
Rabinowitz, get up.
It's 6:00 in the morning.
Your family is today.
Your time to go to the airfield to work, to the airport.
The Germans were building an airport, and thousands of people had to work, taken to that slave labor work.
No, the committee.
They-- The Jewish Committee gave out orders with little, you know, plaques, you know again on houses, who wants to be a Jewish policeman.
So there were a lot, you know, young, strong people, you know, with cool-- good maybe nerves.
And they were not my age.
They were all older, you know, like-- but I don't think if I would be their age, maybe I would have nerve to be a policeman.
I mean they didn't have no weapons, the Jewish police.
They just had like a little police club, a piece of wood, or a piece a leather strap.
But they were powerful enough.
A Jewish policeman could, if you didn't go to work, they could have gone to the committee, and tell Dr.
Elkes or the other people that family refuses to go to work, one day, another day, and then the committee says, listen.
If you got to take a little stronger force, a couple of slaps or a kick, you've got to do it.
I used to work, in the beginning, to go-- we were taken to the airport too, till they established-- and they developed what's called werkstatten, the ghetto werkstatten.
That means working shops, for the Third Reich, for the German Army.
So they developed big factories in the ghetto was established.

And all-- and it was an order, after it was all organized to make all the shops in the ghetto cabinet work, furniture makers, tailors, shoemakers, toy makers, laundry factories to wash for the Germans their laundry, for the Gestapo.
All was established in the ghetto.
And it was given orders by the Jewish Committee, anybody who was a tailor, anybody who was a shoemaker, anybody who was a wood turner, anybody who's a cabinet maker should register to the committee.
And you are going to get an Ausweis, that means a card, a working card that you are working in the ghetto shops, you understand?
So you don't have to go no more to the airfields, airport, to do the hard rough labor work, you understand?
So I registered.
I didn't know too much wood-turning.
But I knew a little already from my father, you know, all the time sneaking in, in the shop.
So I had already a little idea, you know, and I knew a little.
And before we were taken to the ghetto, my father was not in the shop.
I used to go in, all the month and a half, till we went in the ghetto, and I practiced a lot in the shop to forget my problems, to forget my troubles.
So I used to run to the lathe, and do a little, you know, practicing my father's trade, turning.
And, you know what they say?
Wood-turning is a trade.
If your father was a wood turner.
It's in your blood.
It's like you would be born under the lathe.
So I registered to be a wood turner.
And I said to my brother, he was a kid, too, two years younger than me.
I says, Abraham, Abe, Abrahamke let's go register too.
We are not going to-- We'll not have to go anymore to work on the airport.
So we registered, and we registered.
And in the committee was Rabinowitz, my father's friend.
And he gave us the Ausweis, with a number, with everything.
So the police couldn't catch us in the ghetto, in the street, and drag us to the airport or to other certain, you know, the Germans took people to work.
It was not only the airport.
A lot of places the Germans took people from the ghetto to work.
Yes.
And I started work in the werkstatten, in the ghetto shop.
And when I worked in the ghetto shop it was already a better atmosphere for me.
I felt a little more safer.
And all the tailors, my uncles were tailors, they walked in the shops.
And a lot of my friends worked in the shops.
And as a matter of fact, that big man, that leader Rabinowitz, had a son approximately my age, Mr.
Rabinowitz said to me, Maishke, teach my boy a little wood-turning too.
So it was a roll of lathes.
I used to teach him all the time, how to hold a tool, how to cut.
And we were starting to work in the werkstatten.
I was turning parts for toys, wooden toys, airplanes, tanks, trucks, bombs, wooden bombs.
And there was a department of artists in the same werkstatten who did the painting, and put on the swastika, you know, on the tanks, and the wooden toys.
And after the tank was all finished, it looked like a miniature real German tank.
Because we had designers in the werkstatten, Jewish designers, and architects who drew, you know, exactly how the tanks, the toy tanks or the toy trucks, had to be made.
And then it was finished, it was excellent toys.
And the Germans were happy.
And they used to-- they used to pack it in cartons.
And it was shipped to Germany for the German children, and for the German army's kids to play with toys made by the Jews in the ghetto.
You understand?
While our children were killed and slaughtered, we had to make for the German children toys, for them to play in Germany.
And that was heart-breaking.
In the ghetto?
I think about 10 hours or something like that.
In the ghetto, we used to get portions of bread, like for a family, you know, a couple loaves-- in the first months, then it start to get worse, you know.
But the first months, like a couple of loaves of bread.
And in the ghetto was then a kitchen, a soup kitchen.
You could go there and get a bucket.
You know, you have to bring your own dish to get soup, with a lot-- a lot more water than soup, with green leaves.
And sometimes you used to find in the soup a piece of beef, but not from a cow.
It was from horse meat.
